message.content:
I see these in the client.  What are they about?


  └ isGrowthBookEnabled=true
  └ telemetryDisabledBy=none
  └ DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK=unset
  └ hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures=false
  └ growthBookFeaturesLoaded=315
  └ growthBookLastFetched=19h ago
  └ tengu_ccr_bridge=true

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Understand GrowthBook feature flags in client

content.text: These look like feature-flag diagnostics from the Claude Code client (the kind of thing `/doctor` or debug output shows). Let me confirm the specifics with the Claude Code guide agent before explaining.

input.prompt:
A user sees these diagnostic lines in the Claude Code client (likely /doctor or debug output) and wants to know what they mean:

isGrowthBookEnabled=true
telemetryDisabledBy=none
DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK=unset
hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures=false
growthBookFeaturesLoaded=315
growthBookLastFetched=19h ago
tengu_ccr_bridge=true

Please explain: (1) what GrowthBook is and how Claude Code uses it (feature flags / staged rollouts), (2) what each line means, (3) what "tengu" refers to (Claude Code's internal codename?) and if possible what the ccr_bridge flag controls, (4) how a user could disable this if they wanted (env vars like DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK or CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC), and whether any of this sends code/content anywhere. Check official Claude Code docs (https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code) for statsig/growthbook/telemetry env vars. Be precise about what is documented vs. inferred.

content: A more general websearch may be appropriate

content.text:
Based on the documentation I've found, here's what I can tell you about those diagnostic lines. I can confirm some details from official docs, but I need to be explicit about what's documented versus inferred.

---

## What GrowthBook Is and How Claude Code Uses It

**GrowthBook is a feature flagging and experimentation platform.** Claude Code uses it to manage **staged rollouts and A/B tests** of new features — rather than shipping all features to all users at once, GrowthBook lets Anthropic:

- Gate new features to percentages of users
- Run experiments (control vs. treatment groups)
- Kill a feature quickly without deploying
- Vary behavior by user, organization, or account tier

The official documentation confirms this is part of Claude Code's telemetry and configuration system.

---

## What Each Line Means

| Line | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `isGrowthBookEnabled=true` | Feature flag system is **active** — Claude Code will fetch and apply flags. |
| `telemetryDisabledBy=none` | Telemetry is **not disabled** — no environment variable or setting is blocking it. (If you set `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK=1`, this would show `telemetryDisabledBy=env` or similar.) |
| `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK=unset` | The environment variable is **not set**. If you wanted to disable flags, you'd set `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK=1` or `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK=true`. |
| `hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures=false` | The cached feature flags are **stale** — they're from a previous session and haven't been refreshed yet in this session. (Will likely become `true` after the fetch completes.) |
| `growthBookFeaturesLoaded=315` | **315 feature flags** were loaded into memory. This is normal. |
| `growthBookLastFetched=19h ago` | The flag cache was last updated **19 hours ago** — likely from the last session. The client will refresh on startup or periodically. |

---

## What "Tengu" Refers To

**"Tengu" is Claude Code's internal codename.** The official documentation does not detail what subsystems tengu scopes, but the diagnostics show `tengu_ccr_bridge=true`. Based on naming patterns:

- **ccr** likely stands for "Claude Code Router" or similar infrastructure (inference)
- **bridge** suggests it's a data/connectivity layer
- `=true` means it's **active**

This appears to be an **internal telemetry/integration bridge** — probably how Claude Code reports metrics or feature flag results back to Anthropic's infrastructure. **Documented status: not detailed in official docs.**

---

## How to Disable Feature Flags / Telemetry

From the official documentation:

**Option 1: Environment variable** (highest precedence)
```bash
export DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK=1
claude
```

**Option 2: Settings file** (user, project, or local scope)
```json
{
  "env": {
    "DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK": "1"
  }
}
```

Place this in:
- `~/.claude/settings.json` — disables everywhere (user scope)
- `.claude/settings.json` — disables for your project (project scope)
- `.claude/settings.local.json` — disables for you in this project only (local scope, gitignored)

Once disabled, `telemetryDisabledBy` will show `=env` (or the applicable scope), and feature flag fetches will not happen.

---

## Does This Send Code/Content Anywhere?

**Important distinction:**

- **Feature flags themselves**: Small JSON payloads (~few KB) describing which features are enabled for your account. No code, no project content. Purely metadata.
- **Metrics/results**: The `tengu_ccr_bridge` line suggests Claude Code *reports back* results — likely session metadata (success/failure, model used, features exercised), not your actual code or prompts.
- **Official stance**: The docs confirm telemetry can be disabled entirely via `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK`. The specific payloads and destinations are not detailed in public documentation.

**Bottom line**: Feature flags + telemetry do phone home to Anthropic's servers, but are designed to be **strictly non-invasive** (no code/secrets) and **controllable** (one env var disables them). If you work in a restricted environment, setting `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK=1` is the documented way to opt out.

---

## What's Documented vs. Inferred

✓ **Documented (from official Claude Code docs):**
- `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK` environment variable exists and disables feature flag fetching
- GrowthBook is part of the telemetry system
- `telemetryDisabledBy` field shows what disabled it (or `none`)
- Settings can live in `.claude/settings.json`, `.claude/settings.local.json`, or `~/.claude/settings.json`

? **Not documented (inferred from diagnostics):**
- What "tengu" codename refers to internally
- Exact definition of `tengu_ccr_bridge` or what it bridges
- Precise schema/payload size of feature flag syncs
- Retention policy for telemetry

If you need those specifics, `/feedback` inside Claude Code or the GitHub repo (https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code) is the right channel.

duration_ms: 72091</usage>

content.text: The guide agent confirmed the basics but couldn't pin down `tengu_ccr_bridge`. Let me run a broader web search as you suggested.

input.prompt:
Do a general web search (not limited to official Anthropic docs) to find what these Claude Code diagnostic items mean. Search GitHub issues, blog posts, reverse-engineering writeups, Hacker News, Reddit, etc.

Items:
- "isGrowthBookEnabled", "hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures", "growthBookFeaturesLoaded", "growthBookLastFetched", "DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK" in Claude Code
- "tengu" as Claude Code's internal codename (e.g. tengu_ prefixed analytics events / feature flags)
- Specifically: what is "tengu_ccr_bridge"? Note that "CCR" in the Claude Code community often refers to "claude-code-router" (the musistudio/claude-code-router proxy project) — search whether tengu_ccr_bridge relates to that, or to something else (e.g. "Claude Code Remote", "Claude Code on the web"). Try searches like: "tengu_ccr_bridge", "claude code" "ccr_bridge", "claude code" growthbook feature flags list, "tengu_" claude code statsig growthbook.
- Also check whether Claude Code recently migrated from Statsig to GrowthBook for feature flags/telemetry.

Report what you find with sources (URLs), clearly separating confirmed facts from speculation. Be concise.

aiTitle: Understand GrowthBook feature flags in client

content.text:
Research complete. Here is the report, with confirmed facts separated from inference.

## Confirmed facts

**1. GrowthBook diagnostic fields (`isGrowthBookEnabled`, `hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures`, `growthBookFeaturesLoaded`, `growthBookLastFetched`, `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK`)**

Claude Code uses GrowthBook (the open-source feature-flag/AB-testing platform) as its remote feature-flag service. The CLI polls a GrowthBook endpoint roughly hourly and caches flag values (in `~/.claude.json` under `cachedGrowthBookFeatures`). The diagnostic items report the health of that subsystem:

- `isGrowthBookEnabled` — whether the GrowthBook client is active at all; returns false when the `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK` env var is set (or a baseline gate is off), in which case all flag reads fall back to hardcoded defaults.
- `growthBookFeaturesLoaded` — count of flags successfully fetched.
- `hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures` / `growthBookLastFetched` — cache freshness; a failing state looks like `isGrowthBookEnabled=true hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures=false growthBookFeaturesLoaded=0`.
- `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK` is the explicit "no flag traffic, accept frozen defaults" escape hatch. Separately, `DISABLE_TELEMETRY`, `DO_NOT_TRACK`, and `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC` also suppress GrowthBook — a documented complaint, since that silently disables flag-gated features (Agent View, Remote Control, channels) and remote killswitches, not just analytics ([issue #58383](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/58383), [issue #45918](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/45918), [zenn.dev writeup](https://zenn.dev/m0370/articles/d7e77adebd0ba8?locale=en), [gist on the v2.1.76 fix](https://gist.github.com/ahundt/158d498f07a10fec4955a70bf475a20b)).

**2. "tengu" = Claude Code's internal codename**

Well documented from the npm source-map leak and minified-source analyses: `tengu_` is the namespace for Claude Code's entire product control plane — analytics events (`tengu_update_check`, `tengu_oauth_success`, `tengu_mcp_start`), feature gates, killswitches, and operational knobs ([claude-code-decoded TENGU.md](https://github.com/PeronGH/claude-code-decoded/blob/main/TENGU.md), [The Register on the source leak](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/01/claude_code_source_leak_privacy_nightmare/), [hidden-features writeup](https://github.com/sanbuphy/claude-code-source-code/blob/main/docs/en/02-hidden-features-and-codenames.md)).

**3. `tengu_ccr_bridge`**

Confirmed: it is the server-side GrowthBook flag (default `false`) gating **Remote Control** (`/remote-control`) — the bridge that lets claude.ai web/mobile drive a local Claude Code instance. The decompiled logic is essentially `isBridgeEnabled() = getFeatureFlag("tengu_ccr_bridge", false)`, AND-ed with an `allow_remote_sessions` policy check ([issue #34267](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/34267), [#29812](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/29812), [#37140](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/37140), [official docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/remote-control)). A companion flag `tengu_ccr_bridge_multi_session` existed; per the Claude Code Changelog tracker, both were removed around v2.1.66, with `tengu_ccr_bridge` described as "enforces a minimum Claude Code version for Remote Control bridge usage" ([@ClaudeCodeLog](https://x.com/ClaudeCodeLog/status/2029021978288361697)). Known failure modes: the flag flipping false after background GrowthBook cache refresh (fixed by logout/login), and disappearing entirely when telemetry env vars suppress GrowthBook.

**Crucially: it is NOT related to musistudio/claude-code-router.** That community proxy coincidentally shares the "CCR" abbreviation, but `tengu_ccr_bridge` is Anthropic-internal and gates Remote Control. In source analyses, "CCR" names the v2 remote-control transport generation ("v1 WebSocket and v2 SSE+CCR" — asymmetric transport: reads over SSE/WebSocket, writes over HTTP POST) ([zread.ai bridge protocol doc](https://zread.ai/instructkr/claude-code/22-bridge-communication-protocol), [claude-code-from-source ch16](https://claude-code-from-source.com/ch16-remote/)).

**4. Statsig → GrowthBook migration**

Confirmed. Claude Code originally used Statsig for analytics/feature gates; OpenAI acquired Statsig in September 2025, and Anthropic subsequently migrated Claude Code to GrowthBook ([decodethefuture.org](https://decodethefuture.org/en/claude-code-undercover-mode-killswitches-telemetry/), [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/01/claude_code_source_leak_privacy_nightmare/)). Amusingly, GrowthBook itself publishes a Claude Code subagent for migrating Statsig SDK code to GrowthBook ([growthbook/claude-statsig-to-growthbook-sdk-migrator](https://github.com/growthbook/claude-statsig-to-growthbook-sdk-migrator)).

## Speculation / unconfirmed

- **Exact expansion of "CCR"**: no authoritative source spells it out. Community inferences include "Claude Code Remote" (most common, from GitHub issue context) and one search summary's "Claude Cloud Runtime"; the source analyses just treat CCR as the name of the remote-control bridge transport. Anthropic has not published the expansion.
- The claim that the flag flip is caused by device/session-keyed GrowthBook cohort assignment (issue #34267's "Hypothesis B") is user diagnosis, not Anthropic-confirmed.
- The motive for the Statsig→GrowthBook switch (OpenAI acquisition discomfort) is widely repeated but is inference; Anthropic hasn't stated a reason.

Sources: [GH #34267](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/34267) · [GH #29812](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/29812) · [GH #37140](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/37140) · [GH #58383](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/58383) · [GH #45918](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/45918) · [GH #64151](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/64151) · [ClaudeCodeLog tweet](https://x.com/ClaudeCodeLog/status/2029021978288361697) · [zenn.dev](https://zenn.dev/m0370/articles/d7e77adebd0ba8?locale=en) · [ahundt gist](https://gist.github.com/ahundt/158d498f07a10fec4955a70bf475a20b) · [claude-code-decoded TENGU.md](https://github.com/PeronGH/claude-code-decoded/blob/main/TENGU.md) · [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/01/claude_code_source_leak_privacy_nightmare/) · [decodethefuture.org](https://decodethefuture.org/en/claude-code-undercover-mode-killswitches-telemetry/) · [zread.ai bridge protocol](https://zread.ai/instructkr/claude-code/22-bridge-communication-protocol) · [claude-code-from-source ch16](https://claude-code-from-source.com/ch16-remote/) · [growthbook migrator](https://github.com/growthbook/claude-statsig-to-growthbook-sdk-migrator) · [official Remote Control docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/remote-control)

Research complete. Here is the report, with confirmed facts separated from inference.

## Confirmed facts

**1. GrowthBook diagnostic fields (`isGrowthBookEnabled`, `hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures`, `growthBookFeaturesLoaded`, `growthBookLastFetched`, `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK`)**

Claude Code uses GrowthBook (the open-source feature-flag/AB-testing platform) as its remote feature-flag service. The CLI polls a GrowthBook endpoint roughly hourly and caches flag values (in `~/.claude.json` under `cachedGrowthBookFeatures`). The diagnostic items report the health of that subsystem:

- `isGrowthBookEnabled` — whether the GrowthBook client is active at all; returns false when the `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK` env var is set (or a baseline gate is off), in which case all flag reads fall back to hardcoded defaults.
- `growthBookFeaturesLoaded` — count of flags successfully fetched.
- `hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures` / `growthBookLastFetched` — cache freshness; a failing state looks like `isGrowthBookEnabled=true hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures=false growthBookFeaturesLoaded=0`.
- `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK` is the explicit "no flag traffic, accept frozen defaults" escape hatch. Separately, `DISABLE_TELEMETRY`, `DO_NOT_TRACK`, and `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC` also suppress GrowthBook — a documented complaint, since that silently disables flag-gated features (Agent View, Remote Control, channels) and remote killswitches, not just analytics ([issue #58383](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/58383), [issue #45918](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/45918), [zenn.dev writeup](https://zenn.dev/m0370/articles/d7e77adebd0ba8?locale=en), [gist on the v2.1.76 fix](https://gist.github.com/ahundt/158d498f07a10fec4955a70bf475a20b)).

**2. "tengu" = Claude Code's internal codename**

Well documented from the npm source-map leak and minified-source analyses: `tengu_` is the namespace for Claude Code's entire product control plane — analytics events (`tengu_update_check`, `tengu_oauth_success`, `tengu_mcp_start`), feature gates, killswitches, and operational knobs ([claude-code-decoded TENGU.md](https://github.com/PeronGH/claude-code-decoded/blob/main/TENGU.md), [The Register on the source leak](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/01/claude_code_source_leak_privacy_nightmare/), [hidden-features writeup](https://github.com/sanbuphy/claude-code-source-code/blob/main/docs/en/02-hidden-features-and-codenames.md)).

**3. `tengu_ccr_bridge`**

Confirmed: it is the server-side GrowthBook flag (default `false`) gating **Remote Control** (`/remote-control`) — the bridge that lets claude.ai web/mobile drive a local Claude Code instance. The decompiled logic is essentially `isBridgeEnabled() = getFeatureFlag("tengu_ccr_bridge", false)`, AND-ed with an `allow_remote_sessions` policy check ([issue #34267](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/34267), [#29812](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/29812), [#37140](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/37140), [official docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/remote-control)). A companion flag `tengu_ccr_bridge_multi_session` existed; per the Claude Code Changelog tracker, both were removed around v2.1.66, with `tengu_ccr_bridge` described as "enforces a minimum Claude Code version for Remote Control bridge usage" ([@ClaudeCodeLog](https://x.com/ClaudeCodeLog/status/2029021978288361697)). Known failure modes: the flag flipping false after background GrowthBook cache refresh (fixed by logout/login), and disappearing entirely when telemetry env vars suppress GrowthBook.

**Crucially: it is NOT related to musistudio/claude-code-router.** That community proxy coincidentally shares the "CCR" abbreviation, but `tengu_ccr_bridge` is Anthropic-internal and gates Remote Control. In source analyses, "CCR" names the v2 remote-control transport generation ("v1 WebSocket and v2 SSE+CCR" — asymmetric transport: reads over SSE/WebSocket, writes over HTTP POST) ([zread.ai bridge protocol doc](https://zread.ai/instructkr/claude-code/22-bridge-communication-protocol), [claude-code-from-source ch16](https://claude-code-from-source.com/ch16-remote/)).

**4. Statsig → GrowthBook migration**

Confirmed. Claude Code originally used Statsig for analytics/feature gates; OpenAI acquired Statsig in September 2025, and Anthropic subsequently migrated Claude Code to GrowthBook ([decodethefuture.org](https://decodethefuture.org/en/claude-code-undercover-mode-killswitches-telemetry/), [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/01/claude_code_source_leak_privacy_nightmare/)). Amusingly, GrowthBook itself publishes a Claude Code subagent for migrating Statsig SDK code to GrowthBook ([growthbook/claude-statsig-to-growthbook-sdk-migrator](https://github.com/growthbook/claude-statsig-to-growthbook-sdk-migrator)).

## Speculation / unconfirmed

- **Exact expansion of "CCR"**: no authoritative source spells it out. Community inferences include "Claude Code Remote" (most common, from GitHub issue context) and one search summary's "Claude Cloud Runtime"; the source analyses just treat CCR as the name of the remote-control bridge transport. Anthropic has not published the expansion.
- The claim that the flag flip is caused by device/session-keyed GrowthBook cohort assignment (issue #34267's "Hypothesis B") is user diagnosis, not Anthropic-confirmed.
- The motive for the Statsig→GrowthBook switch (OpenAI acquisition discomfort) is widely repeated but is inference; Anthropic hasn't stated a reason.

Sources: [GH #34267](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/34267) · [GH #29812](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/29812) · [GH #37140](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/37140) · [GH #58383](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/58383) · [GH #45918](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/45918) · [GH #64151](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/64151) · [ClaudeCodeLog tweet](https://x.com/ClaudeCodeLog/status/2029021978288361697) · [zenn.dev](https://zenn.dev/m0370/articles/d7e77adebd0ba8?locale=en) · [ahundt gist](https://gist.github.com/ahundt/158d498f07a10fec4955a70bf475a20b) · [claude-code-decoded TENGU.md](https://github.com/PeronGH/claude-code-decoded/blob/main/TENGU.md) · [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/01/claude_code_source_leak_privacy_nightmare/) · [decodethefuture.org](https://decodethefuture.org/en/claude-code-undercover-mode-killswitches-telemetry/) · [zread.ai bridge protocol](https://zread.ai/instructkr/claude-code/22-bridge-communication-protocol) · [claude-code-from-source ch16](https://claude-code-from-source.com/ch16-remote/) · [growthbook migrator](https://github.com/growthbook/claude-statsig-to-growthbook-sdk-migrator) · [official Remote Control docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/remote-control)

These are Claude Code's **feature-flag diagnostics** — they describe the client's connection to GrowthBook, the feature-flag service Anthropic uses to stage rollouts, run A/B tests, and operate remote killswitches. (Claude Code originally used Statsig for this; it migrated to GrowthBook after OpenAI acquired Statsig in late 2025.)

Line by line:

- **`isGrowthBookEnabled=true`** — the flag client is active; nothing (env var or policy) has turned it off.
- **`telemetryDisabledBy=none`** — no setting is suppressing telemetry. The relevant opt-outs are `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK`, `DISABLE_TELEMETRY`, `DO_NOT_TRACK`, and `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC`; if one were set, this line would name it.
- **`DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK=unset`** — the explicit "no flag traffic, use hardcoded defaults" escape hatch isn't set.
- **`hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures=false`** — the cached flag set is stale (consistent with the next two lines).
- **`growthBookFeaturesLoaded=315`** — 315 flags loaded from the local cache (kept in `~/.claude.json`). Normal.
- **`growthBookLastFetched=19h ago`** — last successful fetch from the server; the client polls roughly hourly, so 19h suggests it's been running offline-ish or hasn't refreshed since your last session. It will refresh on its own.

**`tengu_ccr_bridge=true`** is one specific flag. "Tengu" is Claude Code's internal codename — all of its analytics events and feature gates are `tengu_`-prefixed. `ccr_bridge` gates the **Remote Control bridge** — the feature that lets claude.ai on web/mobile drive a local Claude Code instance (`/remote-control`). `true` means it's enabled for you. One caution from the web search: "CCR" here is *not* the community `claude-code-router` proxy — it's Anthropic's name for the remote-control transport (likely "Claude Code Remote"; Anthropic hasn't published the expansion). Per a community changelog tracker, this flag was reportedly removed around v2.1.66, so seeing it suggests your client predates that or it's lingering in the cache.

Privacy-wise: the flag fetch itself is small metadata (which features are on for your account) — none of your code or prompts. If you wanted to opt out, `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK=1` does it, with the documented caveat that it also freezes flag-gated features (Remote Control, channels, etc.) and remote killswitches at their defaults, not just analytics.

In short: routine health output, everything in a normal state except a mildly stale flag cache, which self-heals.

message.content: Yikes, remote kill switches.  That seems to be a scary thing- help me understand it.

content.text:
The phrase sounds more sinister than the mechanism is, but the concern underneath it is legitimate — let me lay out both halves honestly.

**What it actually is.** A "remote killswitch" here is just a feature flag with a defensive purpose. Claude Code ships with all of its code compiled in, including code paths that are off by default. On a regular cadence it asks GrowthBook "which flags are on for this user/version?" and obeys the answer. That gives Anthropic two remote levers: turn a feature *on* gradually (staged rollout), or turn it *off* fleet-wide if it turns out to be broken or dangerous — without waiting for every user to install an update. The `tengu_ccr_bridge` flag you saw is a good concrete example: it gates Remote Control and reportedly enforces a minimum client version for it, so if a serious bug were found in the bridge protocol, Anthropic could flip one flag and every polling client would stop offering the feature within about an hour.

**What it can and cannot do.** This is the part that should defuse most of the scariness:

- A flag is a boolean (or small config value), not code. Flipping one can only select among behaviors *already compiled into the binary you installed*. It is not a channel for pushing new code onto your machine — that's what the updater is, and it's separate.
- The client *pulls* flags; nothing connects inbound to you. If you're offline or block the endpoint, the client falls back to cached values or hardcoded defaults and keeps working.
- You can opt out entirely with `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK=1`, and you can pin a Claude Code version. Frozen defaults plus a pinned binary means nobody can change your tool's behavior remotely at all.

**Why it's not nothing, either.** The honest case for unease has three parts. First, your tool's behavior can change between two runs with no update, no changelog entry, and no notice — a feature you rely on can vanish because a flag flipped (there are real GitHub issues about Remote Control disappearing exactly this way after a cache refresh). Second, the same machinery does cohort assignment, meaning you can be silently placed in an A/B experiment. Third — and this is the one that drew documented criticism — the opt-out coupling is awkward: setting privacy-oriented variables like `DISABLE_TELEMETRY` or `DO_NOT_TRACK` also suppressed flag fetching, which silently disabled flag-gated features *and* removed you from killswitch protection, conflating "don't track me" with "freeze my feature set." That's a design wart Anthropic has been called out on (issue #58383 among others) and has partially addressed.

**The frame I'd offer.** This is the same architecture as Chrome, VS Code, iOS, and basically every modern auto-updating client — remote configuration is how vendors do safe rollouts and emergency response at fleet scale. The killswitch is mostly *for* you: it's the mechanism by which a vulnerability in, say, the remote-control bridge gets neutralized in an hour instead of lingering on every machine that hasn't updated. The cost is a standing trust relationship: you're trusting Anthropic's flag service the same way you already trust their binary and their API. If your threat model includes "vendor changes my tooling underneath me" — which for some of your work it reasonably might — the controls are `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK=1`, version pinning, and egress filtering, with the understood tradeoff that you're then running frozen defaults and own your own emergency response.

lastPrompt: Yikes, remote kill switches.  That seems to be a scary thing- help me understand it.

aiTitle: Understand GrowthBook feature flags in client
